Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

PURPOSE

This manual provides staff with guidance and identifies the standards associated with the investigation of child abuse in Western Australia. The content complements WA Police Investigation Practices (1.0).

It is important therefore that the content of this manual is read in conjunction with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine and not viewed in isolation. In cases where sex offences are involved it is essential to consult the WAPOL Manual for Sex Assault which provides additional investigative guidance in the areas of forensic medical examination and suspect motivational factors.

It is important for all staff engaged in the investigation of sexual offences to be fully conversant with this manual and ensure they are referencing a contemporary version.

In respect of offences committed within the Perth metropolitan area the majority will be dealt with by the Child Abuse Squad (CAS) in line with the terms of their Charter (see Appendix ‘A’). However there are some offences which are the responsibility of the district detective‟s office. In regional WA the responsibility for the investigation of offences is reversed in that most cases will be dealt with by district detectives with primacy only passing to the Child Abuse Squad following agreement between the two business units taking into consideration the seriousness or complexity of an individual case. Contained in this manual is a Table of Responsibilities detailed within the attachment (see Appendix ‘B)’.

Any feedback or recommendations pertaining to this manual should be directed to the Investigative Practices Unit.

SCOPE

This manual relates to offences committed against children as defined in the Western Australia Criminal Code.

The investigation of any offence within this category must be conducted with due consideration to the advice and guidance contained within:

The Child Abuse Squad Charter (Appendix ‘A’);

Table of Responsibilities (Appendix ‘B’);

INTRODUCTION

All investigations conducted into offences of child abuse will be in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine with particular reference to:

Appointment of an Investigating Officer (IO) or where required a Senior Investigating Officer (SIO);

Application of the CRIME Model;

Formulation of strategies under The5KIS;

Categorisation of the investigation; and

Prioritisation of the investigation.

Some investigations will overlap in terms of ownership by the relevant business unit. Officers should refer to the Table of Responsibilities (‘Appendix B’) where primacy for the investigation is not clear and always consult with the Officer in Charge of the relevant business unit (s) for guidance.

sections.h:
Some 3000 cases of child abuse are reported within Western Australia every year. Of those around one third of reports result in a criminal investigation some 80% of which are historic in nature in that the offence was committed some time previously in proximity to the time of reporting. In the majority of cases the offender has formed some relationship, however brief, with the victim. In many cases they will be directly related.

In terms of seriousness offences range from relatively minor physical assault through to serious physical, sexual assault and homicide. The seriousness of any offence of child abuse cannot be overstated with the potential for unchecked minor abuse leading to more serious levels of offending. This notion is reflected in the introduction of mandatory reporting in the amendment to the Children and Community Services Act 2004 in January 2009.

Motivation for offending can range from overzealous chastisement of a child through to gratuitous and in some cases organised paedophilia. For these reasons it is important that any suspicions of child abuse are investigated with the same rigour as more overt cases irrespective of the source of the report.

The difficulty in identifying any signs and symptoms of abuse other than the presence of overt physical injury cannot be overstated. No specific behavioural syndromes characterise victims of sexual abuse. Sexual abuse involves a wide range of possible behaviours which appear to have widely varying effects on its victims. The absence of any sexualised behaviour does not confirm that sexual abuse did not take place any more than the presence of sexuali sed behaviour Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

conclusively demonstrates that sexual abuse occurred; rather, both pieces of information affect the level of suspicion concerning the child's possible experiences and should serve to promote careful impartial investigation.

In some cases the abuse can be of a more subtle nature including emotional abuse and neglect as a form of punishment or for self gratification. It is therefore incumbent on officers to be aware of the signs and symptoms of abuse when carrying out their normal duties.

There is a wealth of national and international research identifying the significant impacts of cumulative harm on a child‟s wellbeing. In conjunction with this research child protection service providers acknowledge that child involvement in domestic violence is a significant risk factor in predicting harm to the child. There is also a connection between domestic violence involving children and the escalation of that violence into incidents of serious harm or homicide. As police are often the first point of contact for women and their children involved in domestic violence they have a significant role to play in preventing violence through positive intervention and effective risk assessme nt. The Australian Institute of Criminology has determined 84% of homicide offenders were related to their child victims as a parent.

WAPOL has implemented a policy to refer all children involved in domestic violence to the Department of Child Protection (DCP) via Family Protection Units which are located in each police district. This is to ensure information holdings about these at-risk children are centrally stored and able to be accessed and investigated by experts in assessment of child harm.

Child Assessment and Interview Team (CAIT)

CAIT was formed on the 1 March 2009 and is located at 79 Stirling Street Perth. It is part of the Sex Crime Division and provides assistance to police operations within the state and throughout Australia. Through the collaboration of WAPOL and DCP the primary role of the work unit is to provide an interview service for all children who have been subject to child abuse.

CAIT has the approved status of a File Manager with respect to child sexual abuse offences within the state of Western Australia. Therefore all child sexual abuse investigations must be assessed by CAIT, in consultation with the Operations Manager CAS before allocation. Failure to do so may impact on the quality of service and responsiveness provided by WAPOL.

The unit captures, analyses, evaluates and distributes all available criminal information in relation to sexual abuse of children using a comprehensive data management system and by conducting relevant research through its extensive information network. Through a multi-agency approach the commitment of Investigative Practices Unit

WAPOL and DCP is to safeguard and promote the wellbeing of children impacted by child abuse.

CAIT is staffed with police officers and DCP personnel who are trained to interview children who have experienced or witnessed offences as defined by Schedule 7 of the Evidence Act 1906. In order to ensure that interviews and their allocations are correctly assigned a Charter of Responsibility has been established.

CAIT is responsible for:

1. Electronically recorded interviews of children in the metropolitan area who

have experienced sexual and/or serious physical abuse as outlined in Schedule 7, Evidence Act 1906;

2. Providing assistance by way of staff and advice to specialist child

interviewers and detectives state-wide;

3. Providing Specialist Child Interviewing and refresher courses state-wide.

CAIT provides specialist child interviewing services for the Child Abuse Squad, Sex Assault Squad and district detectives state-wide. It will provide an after hour response to the investigations within their charter of responsibility as directed by the Officer in Charge of CAIT.

Changes to reporting procedures

On 1 January 2009, the legislation that governs mandatory reporting of child sexual abuse became part of the Children and Community Services Act 2004.

This legislation placed a legal requirement on police officers, doctors, nurses, midwives and teachers to report suspected child sexual abuse to the DCP.

To enable WAPOL to ensure that all officers fulfil their obligations as mandatory reporters CAIT has developed a reporting process to submit reports on behalf of individual officers. Additionally the Officers in Charge and the Operations Managers at CAS and CAIT have been declared as approved persons to receive reports on behalf of DCP.

Since the introduction of mandatory reporting there has been an increase of abuse reports in WA:

In 2008, 1066 reports were received. In 2009, 2427 reports were received and in 2010, 3371 reports were received an increase of 216% since 2008.

As at 31st August 2011, WAPOL have received 2163 reports, a similar number to this time last year.

sections.h:
The application of the CRIME model focuses on the five key areas of:

Contact

Respond

Investigate (This is addressed under The5KIS)

Management

Evaluation

CONTACT

This function may often be carried out by a police staff member in a call handling or station office role. Guidance contained within the WA Police Manual should be used as a reference source.

The initial contact officer (ICO) should advise the person police are there to assist with the matter and should obtain details from the victim (caller) to determine:

The welfare of the victim;

Minimise the potential for loss of evidence; and

The correct resource response to the matter.

Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

When obtaining the information the ICO should give the victim as much privacy as possible and limit questions to the following issues:

Need for medical assistance and personal safety of the victim;

Identity, location and description of the suspect (if known);

Time of the offence in order to prioritise action; and

Location of the incident scene(s);

In relation to the engagement of specialist resources the ICO must refer the allegation to the CAS who will carry out a risk assessment in line with the four phase prioritisation criteria contained within their Charter. Out of the normal working hours of the CAS they have an on-call CAS detective available for advice as required. Where there is any doubt as to the engagement of the CAS, call handlers should err on the side of caution. It is better to consult the CAS at this stage rather than make a decision which can have far reaching consequences later in the investigation.

RESPOND

In common with the majority of incidents is the initial response will be provided by district level uniformed personnel. There may be, however, cases where owing to the circumstances of the allegation more discretion is required when determining the style of response. For example where the alleged offender is a family member and where the child is not in immediate danger it may be more appropriate to delay the mode of response in negotiation with the person reporting. Recourse to an overt uniformed response should only be made after consideration of all of the known facts surrounding the case.

In all cases the ICO must advise the Officer in Charge or the on call member of CAS of the allegation via the Police Operations Centre (POC) or on the CAS on call number. This is particularly the case for those incidents reported in regional WA where investigative ownership sits at district level.

When a report is received by CAIT the circumstances must be subject to a risk assessment and priority allocation from CAIT Operations Manager and ChildFIRST DCP Duty Officer.

In terms of the level of response at the outset the priority is the welfare of the child. The level of response must be in line with the associated risks to the victim in line with Priority 1-4 contained within the Child Abuse Squad Charter (Appendix

‘A’).

When an offence of child abuse is reported the degree of severity and any complicating features will affect the eventual allocation of the investigation in terms of the relevant business unit.

Multi-Agency Strategy Meetings

A strategy meeting is arranged between DCP and WAPOL with other stakeholders invited as required. This meeting will discuss actions and participants reach agreement on the way to proceed. Meetings are usually attended by representatives from DCP, Police, Education, Health, Mandatory Reporter and any other agency appropriate to the circumstances. The d ecisions made during the strategy meeting/discussion should be clearly documented .

The time lapse from the first report to CAIT to the meeting should be no more than 24 hours.

What are the objectives of a strategy meeting?

Clarity about the objectives is important:

to ensure effective multi-agency work by involving people from key agencies;

to provide a rapid response for children in state care;

to facilitate the lawful exchange of information between agencies;

to reach a shared view about how serious the alleged abuse may be;

to establish a common understanding about the overall breadth of the investigation required;

to agree on tasks and timings for each stage of the investigation;

to plan jointly how to carry out the investigation and prepare an action plan;

to agree upon a way of monitoring/reviewing the implementation of the investigation plans;

to identify and implement an emergency protection plan to protect any child pending the outcome of an assessment.

to agree on resources required for regional responses.

Content of the Strategy Meeting

The following issues will need to be considered at a strategy meeting:

What are the wishes, if known, of the child involved?

Is there a need to consider the continuing safety of the child? For example, is there a need for immediate protective action either on a voluntary basis or through the courts?

How can family or carers be involved? Who should be interviewed, what is the best place and time for interviews?

The need to preserve evidence;

In criminal investigations, the child should be considered for an investigative interview (Visually Recorded Interview) or statement in accordance with the procedures;

How can information about the child best be gathered?

Is there a need for a medical examination?

When and how should the child and/or their carers be involved in relevant meetings? How does their current level of distress affect their involvement?

Should they be present at meetings or are there other ways they can contribute to decision making?

What practical assistance would facilitate the child‟s involvement and co - operation; for example, transport to a clinic and interviews, assistance with childcare arrangements, interpreter?

What personal support do families need; for example, links with support groups, separate workers for different family members?

What arrangements should be made to facilitate the involvement and contribution of children with disabilities; for example, conducting interviews in buildings with easy access, the use of interpreters and specialist staff?

Are there issues of race, culture, language or gender that require special arrangements to be made?

Should a worker known to the child/adult/carers/family undertake the Child Assessment Interview, or should a „neutral‟ worker do this and should tasks be shared by more than one worker?

Is it possible that there are other victims?

Consideration will need to be given to when and how the alleged perpetrator is made aware of the allegations. Considerat ion will also need to be given as to who is best placed to notify them of this investigation.

Regional Investigations

Regional police districts are responsible for the investigation and management of child abuse offences within their respective districts. To assist with the investigation and response of these offences, CAS provides metropolitan based Regional Response Teams. Where a CAS Regional Response Team is deployed, the costs for that deployment will be met fully by CAS.

Deployment of a CAS Regional Response Team can be initiated by the Sex Crime Division or the relevant regional police district through the Sex Crime Division (SCD) Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG). Out of sessions applications can be submitted verbally through the Sex Crime Division Divisional Office or through consultation with the CAS OIC.

Investigating Officer

The officer appointed to this role has overall responsibility for the investigative approach and should be engaged as soon as practicable following the initial disclosure. Their role includes:

Management of the investigation in accordance with the CRIME model and application of The5KIS;

The completion of immediate actions including:

Ensuring the victim‟s welfare and medical needs are met;

Assessing the account from the victim to determine investigative strategies;

Assessing the scenes (including the victim, location(s) and suspect);

Ensure preservation of forensic evidence where subsequent examination is required;

Identifying any witnesses or methods of tracing witnesses Arrest suspect(s) on suspicion, if required;

Ensure accurate records of potential evidentiary issues (e.g. first complaint);

Ensure accurate records are maintained regarding investigative actions and decisions.

INVESTIGATE

All investigations will be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine with strict adherence to the CRIME model and THE5KIS listed below:

Physical Material

Witnesses

Intelligence

Public Awareness

Suspects/Persons of Interest

Physical Material

The construction of a forensic strategy is a key priority for the IO in the early stages of the investigation into offences of child abuse. In most incidents of child abuse it is likely that, at some point, legitimate access to the victim or their home has been gained by a suspect, and that the suspect may be living with, or has lived with, the victim as a parent or carer, relative or visitor. This pr esents different issues from those where the suspect is unidentified or has no legitimate access to the scene. In cases where the suspect has had legitimate access, it will not usually be sufficient to identify the suspect as being present at the scene fro m forensic evidence.

The forensic investigation should consider the sequence of events as explained by each party, looking at the scene in that context. This means testing whether the information from the scene corroborates the sequence of events supplied by the suspect as well as the victims and witnesses. This principle may also apply to scenes of previous incidents.

Potential areas of significant evidence which could assist the investigator include:

On arrival at the scene and/or place of reporting it is important to note the demeanour of the complainant, particularly in those cases where the person reporting is the victim. Initial interaction with the person reporting should focus on the twin imperatives of:

Victim welfare, and

Securing and preservation of evidence in accordance with The5KIS.

Opportunities to obtain physical material at this stage may be limited but could include:

Photographs of any obvious injuries;

Consideration of use of Preliminary Evidence Kit in sexual abuse cases;

Description of physical appearance and condition of the victim;

Observations of living conditions;

Access to computer equipment and internet particularly in sexual abuse cases;

Medical history of the victim.

With regard to the physical examination of the victim, early consideration must be given to the need for a specially trained doctor and/or paediatrician to be engaged so the appropriate skills are made available to investigators.

Princess Margaret Hospital Child Protection Unit

Princess Margaret Hospital Child Protection Unit (PMH CPU) carries out assessments of children when there is a concern they have, or may have, suffered from abuse. PMH CPU provides medical, forensic, social work and therapeutic services, along with training and education of other professionals working with children. The CPU is staffed by paediatricians, senior social workers and therapists.

Medical Service

Medical examination and opinion

Diagnosis and treatment of injuries and infections

Medical investigations, including blood tests and imaging Investigative Practices Unit

Therapy Service

Offers a range of therapy services to children, adolescents and their families where abuse has occurred

Therapy services include individual, group and family therapy Witnesses

In intra familial child abuse cases witnesses may emanate from a number of sources including family members, friends and carers. In sexual abuse cases it must be noted that a witness might also be the person providing evidence of early complaint for evidential purposes. All relevant procedures for the securing of witness evidence including any special legal provisions must be observed.

All witness interviews must be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Investigative Interviewing Regime and by officers trained to the relevant standard.

Forensic Child Interview

The below objectives underpin the WAPOL joint response with DCP in conducting an assessment of a child‟s environment and suitability to subjecting the child to a Forensic Child Interview. The joint approach seeks to:

Provide a standard systematic approach in conducting interviews with children who have experienced sexual and/or physical abuse;

Provide a framework within which to conduct interviews;

Provide standards of qualifications of Specialist Child Interviews in accordance with Section 106HA of the Evidence Act;

Provide a state-wide quality assurance process of visually recorded child interviews;

Enhance the professionalism within which interviews are conducted;

Provide integrity to interviews that may come under judicial scrutiny;

Promote accountability of interviews;

Promote confidence in members attached to the unit when confronted with complex, high profile interviews.

Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)

Officers are reminded that victims may be suffering from the effects of Post Traumatic Stress Disorder which may affect their ability/willingness to recall exact details of the abuse. For this reason the securing of victim testimony may be necessary over an extended period of time.

It is long established practice the obtaining of victim testimony must be carried out by specially trained officers within the Child Abuse Interview Trained (CAIT) cadre in the metropolitan area and by suitably trained personnel in regional WA.

First Complaint Evidence

In some child abuse cases there will be an element of sexual abuse. In such cases it is necessary for officers to be cognisant of the need to address the notion of „First Complaint‟. Evidence of first complaint is an exception to the rules of hearsay which relates to the first person(s) the victim informed as to the nature of the offence. This can be soon after the assault or as soon as practicable thereafter. In practice the disclosure of the assault can be sometime after the event, in some cases a number of years. More commonly the first complaint is made relatively soon after the assault.

In evidential terms it is important to secure this disclosure as research suggests the content of the disclosure is often of significant evidential value for the furtherance of the investigation.

When obtaining evidence of first complaint officers should record the following information from the witness:

The circumstances in which disclosure took place including location and method i.e. in person/by telephone;

Victim‟s disclosure – verbatim;

Any witnesses to the first complaint;

The victim‟s demeanour. Include the state of dress, physical injuries or conditions such as crying or hysteria; and

If the report was made to POC or the 000 service an extract of the call should be secured. The officer receiving the call could give evidence of first complaint and a statement should be taken from the officer as soon as practicable.

Where the victim has related the circumstances of the assault to a numbe r of different people and there is confusion as to which person is the first complaint witness, interviews should be conducted with those persons and statements Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

obtained. In the event of a prosecution the ODPP should be consulted in order to establish which person is the first complaint witness in law.

General Witness

Witnesses in this category can include those who can provide testimony for continuity, background, intelligence and alibi.

In all cases the requirement to show continuity in exhibit handling is an important feature. This is particularly so in sex assault cases where the potential for cross contamination is heightened owing to the nature of the evidence.

An additional feature of these assaults is the requirement to confirm the alleged offender had the opportunity to commit the crime in the sense there is no evidence to suggest the suspect was in a different place at the time of the assault. Where a suspect gives notice of alibi, investigators m ust go to great lengths to ensure the alibi provided is thoroughly investigated before a suspect can be eliminated from the enquiry.

Expert Witnesses

An additional feature of child abuse investigations is the use of expert witnesses such as doctors, forensic scientists and psychologists to interpret evidential findings. In all cases involving their employment cognisance should be taken of the experience and qualifications when formulating a statement of evidence.

Intelligence

Developing strategies to obtain relevant sources of information include the following;

Entering relevant intelligence on the IMS;

In cases where the identity of the offender is unknown cross check intelligence data bases in the areas of:

o Crime profile of the area;

o Any precursor offences;

o Known offenders in the area;

o Linked crimes;

Contact should be made with the State Intelligence Division (SID) to allow the allocation of an analyst to assist with intelligence related investigative actions.

Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

Suspects/Persons of Interest

The investigative strategies used for a child abuse offence suspect should be well planned and demonstrate an awareness of the potential motivations, the need to avoid contamination/loss of physical material and or the potential of putting the victim or witnesses at risk.

It is important for investigating officers to build a profile of suspect behaviour leading to and following the offence. Areas to cover with the suspect include:

• Circumstances leading up to the alleged offence;

• Motive when committing the alleged offence;

• Character of the suspect;

• Previous child abuse by the suspect;

• General relationships with children and child contact arrangements, if

applicable;

• Nature of other relationships, past and present, including history of abuse

and controlling

• Behaviour on the part of the suspect (eg, domestic violence);

• Relationship with other family members, family structure and functioning;

• Any circumstances where children of the family lived away from home;

• Contact with external support agencies;

• Parenting style, where applicable;

• Self care and care of other family members, where applicable;

• Significant family events, such as deaths and illnesses;

• Issues relating to grooming such as membership of children‟s organisations

and fostering of interests in children‟s websites;

• Other abuse towards strangers and acquaintances;

• Any drug or alcohol misuse issues.

Interview of the Suspect

All suspect interviews must be carried out within the auspices of the PEACE model. Due to the nature of child abuse investigations particular attention should be paid to planning and preparing for a suspect interview.

Where there is no direct evidence importance should be attached to corroborating the account of the victim. For example placing the complainant and person of interest together at particular places, on particulars times or dates. The same importance should be attached to any age issues where the nature of the charge is predicated on the age of the victim. For example the suspect should be asked to provide an explanation as to why he believed the victim was of a certain age.

Consideration should be given to engaging a qualified Tier 4 Interview Advisor during this phase if appropriate.

Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

Bail Considerations

In child abuse investigations there is often some form of relationship between the suspect and victim. Many offences take place in the home and between p arties who are co-habiting. For these reasons it is important to emphasise the importance of ensuring post charge contact between the two parties and key witnesses is prevented by appropriate imposition of bail conditions.

In reaching a decision on the application for bail conditions investigators should consider the following influencing factors:

Associated risks to the victim or future victims;

Protective conditions;

Surety bail;

Violence Restraining Orders in domestic violence cases.

Public Awareness

Investigating Officers are reminded of the need for confidentiality in most cases of child abuse so the anonymity of relevant parties is protected. Officers should use the WAPOL Investigation Doctrine as a reference source for potential strategies.

MANAGEMENT

There is always the potential for child abuse cases to involve an element of risk to the victim or future victims as well as the loss of physical evidence. It is important therefore in managing the investigation, consideration is given to the following:

Timeliness of the investigation and ensuring immediate response where required; and

Managing the associated risks to the victim and to the agency;

The allocation of a child abuse investigation must be in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine and the Child Abuse Squad Charter (see Appendix ‘A’) and Table of Responsibilities (see Appendix ‘B’). It is essential a CRIME file is commenced in all circumstances and an Investigation Action Register maintained to assist with the management of the investigation. In complex investigations consideration should be given to utilising Viper case management.

There may be an attendant requirement to engage with other departments to ensure awareness of the investigation is raised in a wider a rena so associated risks are minimised. This includes other departments within WAPOL but also external partners such as the Department of Child Protection, PMH CPU and victim support agencies (see section on Follow up Victim Services).

Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

For example where the incident is linked to a family violence incident the WA Police Family Violence Co-ordinator for the district where the incident occurred should be consulted and advised of the circumstances. Local Family Violence Coordinator can then take proactive measures to assist the victim in relation to their personal safety.

In November 2010, DV-1.1.4.3 (IMS) policy was updated to standardise information recorded on domestic violence incident reports (DVIR‟s). Refer to DVIR 1-9. This information included identifying children who were either present at the incident or usually resided with the parties. It also included recording an y identified risk and behavioural factors which would assist both WAPOL and DCP with risk assessment. (See Appendix C)

For all incidents attended / reported involving persons in a Family and Domestic Relationship (FDR) it is mandatory an Incident Report (IR) is submitted. Members IR‟s must indicate the existence of the FDR by checking the FDR field on the initial attendance screen (Y/N). Activation of this field will assist with the recording of his / her actions as required by section 62C of the restraining Orders Act 1997.

Child Witness Service

The Child Witness Service helps children and young people under 18 years of age who may need to give evidence in court. The children involved can be victims or witnesses to any criminal charge, in any court. Practical information is provided to help children and young people emotionally prepare for court. Support is provided on an individual basis. Evidence is never discussed.

It is important children are referred to the Child Witness Service as soon as possible after charges have been laid against an alleged offender. It is not necessary to know for certain if the child is required to give evidence. Child victims and witnesses can receive support from the time charges are laid until when the matter is finalised in court.

Victim Support Services

Counsellors at the PMH CPU provide counselling support to children affected by previous child abuse violence. People can be referred for ongoing individual or group counselling following an emergency service. They can also be referred by other agencies or through self-referral.

The Victim Support Service (VSS) will also provide assistance to victims of crime and in particular to victims of violent personal crime. The IO following contact with the victim‟s parent/guardian should advise the VSS of the details of the victim and the circumstances surrounding the incident including details of any person charged.

Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

This needs to be done with the consent of the victim‟s parent/guardian. Once advised the VSS will make contact with the victim and offer support and services.

Contact should be made with the VSS as soon as possible after the incident to allow that agency to provide the appropriate support.

Intrusive Supervision

In terms of the timeliness of the investigation it is important to ensure supervision of each case is dynamic so ongoing evaluation as to the progress of the investigation is maintained. Many child abuse cases are reliant on the continuing engagement of the victim where any apparent tardiness to the pace of the investigation can have a negative effect on victim confidence.

It is necessary to maintain close supervision of investigations through structured case management procedures to ensure the efficacy of the investigation.

Supervisors should ensure that in all investigations the relevant case management processes are completed and documented.

EVALUATE

Case Supervision

Maintaining victim confidence is a key factor in sex offence investigation s. The trauma and anxiety intrinsic in the victims experience can be aggravated in cases which, to them, appear to be long and drawn out, with the potential to add to the angst.

It is essential therefore all investigations into sex offences are conducted in a timely manner which maintains victim confidence and also ensures the matter has been subject to a thorough investigation. There is a requirement for supervisors to monitor the pace and the effectiveness of the investigation so the se two imperatives are delivered.

All incidents of Child Abuse related offences will be subject to evaluation under the auspices of the WA Police Investigative Doctrine in relation to its resolution, any endorsement to charge and subsequent review process.

In terms of multi agency action plans and strategies relevant evaluation criteria should be set at the outset ensuring flexibility to meet changing demands.

Case review

The reviewing of all cases will be in accordance with the WA Police Crime Review Policy.

sections.b: The application of the CRIME model focuses on the five key areas of: Contact Respond Investigate (This is addressed under The5KIS) Management Evaluation CONTACT This function may often be carried out by a police staff member in a call handling or station office role. Guidance contained within the WA Police Manual should be used as a reference source. The initial contact officer (ICO) should advise the person police are there to assist with the matter and should obtain details from the victim (caller) to determine: The welfare of the victim; Minimise the potential for loss of evidence; and The correct resource response to the matter. Investigative Practices Unit Version 1.0 Specialist Investigation Manual Child Abuse When obtaining the information the ICO should give the victim as much privacy as possible and limit questions to the following issues: Need for medical assistance and personal safety of the victim; Identity, location and description of the suspect (if known); Time of the offence in order to prioritise action; and Location of the incident scene(s); In relation to the engagement of specialist resources the ICO must refer the allegation to the CAS who will carry out a risk assessment in line with the four phase prioritisation criteria contained within their Charter. Out of the normal working hours of the CAS they have an on-call CAS detective available for advice as required. Where there is any doubt as to the engagement of the CAS, call handlers should err on the side of caution. It is better to consult the CAS at this stage rather than make a decision which can have far reaching consequences later in the investigation. RESPOND In common with the majority of incidents is the initial response will be provided by district level uniformed personnel. There may be, however, cases where owing to the circumstances of the allegation more discretion is required when determining the style of response. For example where the alleged offender is a family member and where the child is not in immediate danger it may be more appropriate to delay the mode of response in negotiation with the person reporting. Recourse to an overt uniformed response should only be made after consideration of all of the known facts surrounding the case. In all cases the ICO must advise the Officer in Charge or the on call member of CAS of the allegation via the Police Operations Centre (POC) or on the CAS on call number. This is particularly the case for those incidents reported in regional WA where investigative ownership sits at district level. When a report is received by CAIT the circumstances must be subject to a risk assessment and priority allocation from CAIT Operations Manager and ChildFIRST DCP Duty Officer. In terms of the level of response at the outset the priority is the welfare of the child. The level of response must be in line with the associated risks to the victim in line with Priority 1-4 contained within the Child Abuse Squad Charter (Appendix ‘A’). When an offence of child abuse is reported the degree of severity and any complicating features will affect the eventual allocation of the investigation in terms of the relevant business unit. Investigative Practices Unit Version 1.0 Specialist Investigation Manual Child Abuse Multi-Agency Strategy Meetings A strategy meeting is arranged between DCP and WAPOL with other stakeholders invited as required. This meeting will discuss actions and participants reach agreement on the way to proceed. Meetings are usually attended by representatives from DCP, Police, Education, Health, Mandatory Reporter and any other agency appropriate to the circumstances. The d ecisions made during the strategy meeting/discussion should be clearly documented . The time lapse from the first report to CAIT to the meeting should be no more than 24 hours. What are the objectives of a strategy meeting? Clarity about the objectives is important: to ensure effective multi-agency work by involving people from key agencies; to provide a rapid response for children in state care; to facilitate the lawful exchange of information between agencies; to reach a shared view about how serious the alleged abuse may be; to establish a common understanding about the overall breadth of the investigation required; to agree on tasks and timings for each stage of the investigation; to plan jointly how to carry out the investigation and prepare an action plan; to agree upon a way of monitoring/reviewing the implementation of the investigation plans; to identify and implement an emergency protection plan to protect any child pending the outcome of an assessment. to agree on resources required for regional responses. Content of the Strategy Meeting The following issues will need to be considered at a strategy meeting: What are the wishes, if known, of the child involved? Is there a need to consider the continuing safety of the child? For example, is there a need for immediate protective action either on a voluntary basis or through the courts? How can family or carers be involved? Who should be interviewed, what is the best place and time for interviews? The need to preserve evidence; In criminal investigations, the child should be considered for an investigative interview (Visually Recorded Interview) or statement in accordance with the procedures; How can information about the child best be gathered? Is there a need for a medical examination? Investigative Practices Unit Version 1.0 Specialist Investigation Manual Child Abuse When and how should the child and/or their carers be involved in relevant meetings? How does their current level of distress affect their involvement? Should they be present at meetings or are there other ways they can contribute to decision making? What practical assistance would facilitate the child‟s involvement and co - operation; for example, transport to a clinic and interviews, assistance with childcare arrangements, interpreter? What personal support do families need; for example, links with support groups, separate workers for different family members? What arrangements should be made to facilitate the involvement and contribution of children with disabilities; for example, conducting interviews in buildings with easy access, the use of interpreters and specialist staff? Are there issues of race, culture, language or gender that require special arrangements to be made? Should a worker known to the child/adult/carers/family undertake the Child Assessment Interview, or should a „neutral‟ worker do this and should tasks be shared by more than one worker? Is it possible that there are other victims? Consideration will need to be given to when and how the alleged perpetrator is made aware of the allegations. Considerat ion will also need to be given as to who is best placed to notify them of this investigation. Regional Investigations Regional police districts are responsible for the investigation and management of child abuse offences within their respective districts. To assist with the investigation and response of these offences, CAS provides metropolitan based Regional Response Teams. Where a CAS Regional Response Team is deployed, the costs for that deployment will be met fully by CAS. Deployment of a CAS Regional Response Team can be initiated by the Sex Crime Division or the relevant regional police district through the Sex Crime Division (SCD) Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG). Out of sessions applications can be submitted verbally through the Sex Crime Division Divisional Office or through consultation with the CAS OIC. Investigating Officer The officer appointed to this role has overall responsibility for the investigative approach and should be engaged as soon as practicable following the initial disclosure. Their role includes: Management of the investigation in accordance with the CRIME model and application of The5KIS; The completion of immediate actions including: Ensuring the victim‟s welfare and medical needs are met; Investigative Practices Unit Version 1.0 Specialist Investigation Manual Child Abuse Assessing the account from the victim to determine investigative strategies; Assessing the scenes (including the victim, location(s) and suspect); Ensure preservation of forensic evidence where subsequent examination is required; Identifying any witnesses or methods of tracing witnesses Arrest suspect(s) on suspicion, if required; Ensure accurate records of potential evidentiary issues (e.g. first complaint); Ensure accurate records are maintained regarding investigative actions and decisions. INVESTIGATE All investigations will be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine with strict adherence to the CRIME model and THE5KIS listed below: Physical Material Witnesses Intelligence Public Awareness Suspects/Persons of Interest Physical Material The construction of a forensic strategy is a key priority for the IO in the early stages of the investigation into offences of child abuse. In most incidents of child abuse it is likely that, at some point, legitimate access to the victim or their home has been gained by a suspect, and that the suspect may be living with, or has lived with, the victim as a parent or carer, relative or visitor. This pr esents different issues from those where the suspect is unidentified or has no legitimate access to the scene. In cases where the suspect has had legitimate access, it will not usually be sufficient to identify the suspect as being present at the scene fro m forensic evidence. The forensic investigation should consider the sequence of events as explained by each party, looking at the scene in that context. This means testing whether the information from the scene corroborates the sequence of events supplied by the suspect as well as the victims and witnesses. This principle may also apply to scenes of previous incidents. Potential areas of significant evidence which could assist the investigator include: Investigative Practices Unit Version 1.0 Specialist Investigation Manual Child Abuse • Blood traces • Fingerprints • Medication • Clothing and bedding • Footmarks • Injuries • Weapons • Obvious signs of neglect/abuse On arrival at the scene and/or place of reporting it is important to note the demeanour of the complainant, particularly in those cases where the person reporting is the victim. Initial interaction with the person reporting should focus on the twin imperatives of: Victim welfare, and Securing and preservation of evidence in accordance with The5KIS. Opportunities to obtain physical material at this stage may be limited but could include: Photographs of any obvious injuries; Consideration of use of Preliminary Evidence Kit in sexual abuse cases; Description of physical appearance and condition of the victim; Observations of living conditions; Access to computer equipment and internet particularly in sexual abuse cases; Medical history of the victim. With regard to the physical examination of the victim, early consideration must be given to the need for a specially trained doctor and/or paediatrician to be engaged so the appropriate skills are made available to investigators. Princess Margaret Hospital Child Protection Unit Princess Margaret Hospital Child Protection Unit (PMH CPU) carries out assessments of children when there is a concern they have, or may have, suffered from abuse. PMH CPU provides medical, forensic, social work and therapeutic services, along with training and education of other professionals working with children. The CPU is staffed by paediatricians, senior social workers and therapists. All relevant procedures for the securing of witness evidence including any special legal provisions must be observed. All witness interviews must be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Investigative Interviewing Regime and by officers trained to the relevant standard. The joint approach seeks to: Provide a standard systematic approach in conducting interviews with children who have experienced sexual and/or physical abuse; Provide a framework within which to conduct interviews; Provide standards of qualifications of Specialist Child Interviews in accordance with Section 106HA of the Evidence Act; Provide a state-wide quality assurance process of visually recorded child interviews; Enhance the professionalism within which interviews are conducted; Provide integrity to interviews that may come under judicial scrutiny; Promote accountability of interviews; Investigative Practices Unit Version 1.0 Specialist Investigation Manual Child Abuse Promote confidence in members attached to the unit when confronted with complex, high profile interviews. sections.h:
ODPP Liaison

The Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (ODPP) is the independent prosecuting authority for the State of Western Australia and has appointed a liaison officer for WAPOL. The purpose of this is twofold, firstly to obtain early legal advice in principle as to whether or not a prosecution case exists and secondly, what evidence is needed to further enhance the prospect of a successful prosecution.

Email requests for advice or guidance from the ODPP liaison officer should be directed to the ODPP Police Advice Line via dppadvice@dpp.wa.gov.au Offender’s antecedents

Once an offender is charged and the matter is referred to the ODPP, any previous convictions of a similar nature, or allegations (particularly those that have not resulted in charges), should be brought to the attention of the ODPP file manager as it may be possible to adduce this as propensity evidence under s.31A of the Evidence Act 1906 (WA).

Particularisation

The concept of particularisation arises from the 1989 High Court of Australia decision Shaw v R (89 ALR 321). This concept is particularly relevant with respect to child sex offences. The following points are of particular relevance:

1. The law requires there be certainty as to the particular offence with

which a person is charged.

2. If an offence is not particularised, then admissibility of similar fact

evidence cannot be adequately dealt with.

3. It is unfair for the accused to have to defend himself / herself in

relation to an indeterminate number of occasions, any one of which may constitute the offence for which he/she stands charged, if the offences are not particularised. For example, the defence of alibi would be almost impossible to raise.

4. A jury, faced with unparticularised offences, could reach a verdict

with some jurors convicting / acquitting in relation to one instance whilst other jurors were convicting / acquitting in relation to a different instance.

Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

A prosecution witness must be able to give evidence about a particular occasion from his / her memory and not rely on vague recollections of offences occurring regularly without detail. The witness must be able to specifically recall the event which constitutes the offence/s and be able to distinguish that event from other similar events.

Corroboration

The concept of corroboration arises from the decision in R v Clynes (1960) 44 Cr App R 1558. Corroboration is independent evidence of some fact that confirms the commission of the offence and the identity of the accused as being the person who committed the offence.

As a matter of investigative prudence the IO should ensure all corroborative evidence is collected. Corroboration can be found in a number of area s, the list provided is not exhaustive but are suggested areas for consideration:

House plans

Vehicle registration records

Personal diaries

Medical Evidence

Department of Child Protection case files

Previous criminal convictions

Witness accounts

For further information in relation to particularisation and corroboration refer to the Particularisation and Corroboration Detective Training Précis.

sections.h:
The Child Abuse Squad (CAS) is staffed with specialist investigators but does not have the resources to investigate all crimes against children. It is therefore important they do investigate the most serious, sensitive and protracted inquiries. In order to rationalise and properly manage the correct allocation of investigations a Charter of Responsibility has been established.

The Child Assessment Interview Team (CAIT) has the approved status of a file Manager with respect to all child abuse offences within the State of Western Australia. Therefore all child abuse investigations must be assessed by the CAIT, in consultation with the Operations Manager CAS before allocation; failure to do so may impact on the quality of service and responsiveness provided by the Police Service.

The CAIT is responsible for the initial intake and vetting of child abuse offences.

CAIT and CAS will maintain effective child abuse incident management by ensuring an appropriate and efficient initial response where and when required, having regard to the categorisation and prioritisation of the incident. By exercising appropriate discretion CAIT will, in consultation with the relevant District Detectives and CICU File Manager, ensure prompt identification of cases meriting further investigation by CAS or the relevant District Detectives.

The CAS will support the Districts by being responsible for those child abuse incidents that fall within the CAS Charter of Responsibility, and initiate investigation by CAS. Child abuse offences outside the CAS Charter of Responsibility will be allocated by CAIT to the CICU File Manager of the District in which the victim resides.

The Major Crime Squad has responsibility to investigate any confirmed or suspected non-accidental injury, which has resulted in the death of a child.

DEFINITIONS

Investigative Practices Unit

Version 1.0

Specialist Investigation Manual

Child Abuse

For the purpose of this Charter, the following definitions will apply;

Intra-familial Where the perpetrator has a familial relationship with the child, either by blood, marriage or other familial

connection. Not restricted to lineal relative.

Extra-familial Where the perpetrator does not have a familial relationship with the child, either by blood, marriage or other familial connection.

Serious Injury An injury that constitutes grievous bodily harm or any injury incurred as a result of an assault that could have endangered life (eg. shaking, drowning etc.)

Person in Authority A person who has care, supervision or authority of a child for the purposes of Chapter XXXI Criminal Code.

sections.h:
The following child abuse investigations in the metropolitan area will be investigated and managed by CAS.

Sexual abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting.

Sexual abuse of a child under 13 years of age in an extra-familial setting.

Physical abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting that results in serious injury.

Sexual abuse or physical abuse where the alleged offender is a person in authority.

Sexual offences against children by unknown (stranger) offenders are the responsibility of the Sex Assault Squad. Other types of child abuse investigations remain the responsibility of the Metropolitan Police District in which the complainant resides. These matters include, but are not limited to;

Sexual abuse of a child 13 years and over in an extra-familial setting.

Non-serious physical abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting. For example, sibling assaults, unlawful chastisement, etc.

Serious and non-serious physical abuse of all children in an extra-familial setting. For example peer assaults, bullying or assaults by street gangs.

Historical allegations made by an adult of offences committed upon them as a child.

sections.h:
Regional Police Districts are responsible for the investigation and management of child abuse offences within their respective districts. To assist with the investigation and response of these offences, CAS provides Metropolitan based Regional Response Teams. Where a CAS Regional Response Team is deployed, the costs for that deployment will be met fully by CAS.

Deployment of a CAS Regional Response Team can be initiated by the Sex Crime Division or the relevant Regional Police District through the Sex Crime Division

(SCD) Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG). Out of sessions applications can be

submitted verbally through the Sex Crime Division Divisional office or in consultation with the CAS OIC.

Regional Response Teams will operate on a priority availability basis and as directed in the Regional Response protocol. While each deployment request will be subjectively reviewed refer to Table A below as a guide for assessment priorities.

sections.h:
CAS provides an on-call service through direct contact with an experienced Detective. The on-call details are available by contacting POC or via Child Abuse Squad SMAIL address book located in Outlook. This officer provides a single point of contact for police and other agencies to refer child abuse investigation cases. The on-call officer will provide an advisory service for situations which arise outside normal working hours.

The on-call officer will respond to calls as quickly as possible, prioritising them according to the information available at the time. Calls will be prioritised according to the degree of vulnerability and risk to safety, with injury or further abuse, and dangers to the victim or other children being the highest priorities. The on-call officer will assess the situation or request and will decide if the situation requires the immediate attendance of a CAS team.

Those callers whose incidents do not require immediate attention will be offered appropriate advice and information. The on-call officer will keep a written record of all calls and actions taken and refer appropriately to CAIT.

sections.h:
On 1 January 2009, the legislation that governs mandatory reporting of child sexual abuse became part of the Children and Community Services Act 2004.

This legislation placed a legal requirement on police officers, doctors, nurses, midwives and teachers to report suspected child sexual abuse to the DCP.

To enable WAPOL to ensure all officers fulfil their obligations as mandatory reporters CAIT has developed a reporting process to submit reports on behalf of individual officers. Additionally the Officers in Charge and the Operations Managers at CAS and CAIT have been declared as approved persons to receive reports on behalf of DCP.
